quality.rationale: 完整静态度规假设+多步变分与坐标变换推导+光线传播物理场景

origin.question: Problem 7.14. Prove the general relativity version of Fermat's principle: In any static metric $(\mathbf{g}_{0j} = \mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta,0} = 0)$ , consider all null curves between two points in space, $\mathbf{x}^j = \mathbf{a}^j$ and $\mathbf{x}^j = \mathbf{b}^j$ . Each such null curve $\mathbf{x}^j(t)$ requires a particular coordinate time $\Delta t$ to get from $\mathbf{a}^j$ to $\mathbf{b}^j$ . Show that the curves of extremal time $\Delta t$ are null geodesics of the spacetime.## SolutionSolution 7.14. Start with the geodesic equation$$\frac {\mathrm {d}^{2} \mathbf {x}^{a}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda^{2}} + \Gamma_{\beta \gamma}^{a} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\beta}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\gamma}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} = 0 .$$Change variables from affine parameter $\lambda$ to coordinate time $t$ and use$$0 = d t^{2} + \left(g_{i j} / g_{0 0}\right) d x^{i} d x^{k} \tag {1}$$to get$$\mathbf {g}_{\mathrm {j k}} \frac {\mathrm {d}^{2} \mathbf {x}^{\mathbf {k}}}{\mathrm {d} t^{2}} + \Gamma_{\mathrm {j k l}} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\mathbf {k}}}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\ell}}{\mathrm {d} t} - \Gamma_{\mathrm {j 0 0}} \frac {\mathbf {g}_{\mathrm {k l}}}{\mathbf {g}_{0 0}} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\mathbf {k}}}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\ell}}{\mathrm {d} t} + \frac {\mathrm {d}^{2} \mathrm {t / d} \lambda^{2}}{(\mathrm {d} t / \mathrm {d} \lambda )^{2}} \mathbf {g}_{\mathrm {j k}} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\mathbf {k}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = 0 .$$Combine this with the time part of the geodesic equation$$\frac {(\mathrm {d}^{2} \mathrm {t} / \mathrm {d} \lambda^{2})}{(\mathrm {d t} / \mathrm {d} \lambda )^{2}} = - 2 \Gamma_{0 \mathrm {k} 0} \frac {\mathrm {d x}^{\mathrm {k}} / \mathrm {d t}}{\mathbf {g}_{0 0}}$$and use the expression for the $\Gamma$ 's in terms of the metric to get$$\gamma_{j k} \frac {\mathrm {d}^{2} x^{k}}{\mathrm {d} t^{2}} + \frac {1}{2} \left(\gamma_{j k, l} + \gamma_{j l, k} - \gamma_{k l, j}\right) \frac {\mathrm {d} x^{k}}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {\mathrm {d} x^{\ell}}{\mathrm {d} t} = 0, \tag {2}$$where $\gamma_{jk} \equiv -g_{jk} / g_{00}$ . Notice that this is just a geodesic equation with affine parameter $t$ in the 3-dimensional manifold with metric $\gamma_{jk}$ . From Equation (1) it is clear then that the solution of Equation (2) extremizes $\int dt$ ; Fermat's principle holds. [See also C. Moller, The Theory of Relativity (Oxford University Press, 2nd ed., 1972), p. 308.]
origin.answer: $\Delta t$ 取极值的空间曲线满足光学度规 $\gamma_{jk} \equiv -g_{jk} / g_{00}$ 下的三维测地线方程，即为四维时空的零测地线（Fermat 原理成立）。
origin.solution: Start with the geodesic equation$$\frac {\mathrm {d}^{2} \mathbf {x}^{a}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda^{2}} + \Gamma_{\beta \gamma}^{a} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\beta}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\gamma}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} = 0 .$$Change variables from affine parameter $\lambda$ to coordinate time $t$ and use$$0 = d t^{2} + \left(g_{i j} / g_{0 0}\right) d x^{i} d x^{k} \tag {1}$$to get$$\mathbf {g}_{\mathrm {j k}} \frac {\mathrm {d}^{2} \mathbf {x}^{\mathbf {k}}}{\mathrm {d} t^{2}} + \Gamma_{\mathrm {j k l}} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\mathbf {k}}}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\ell}}{\mathrm {d} t} - \Gamma_{\mathrm {j 0 0}} \frac {\mathbf {g}_{\mathrm {k l}}}{\mathbf {g}_{0 0}} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\mathbf {k}}}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\ell}}{\mathrm {d} t} + \frac {\mathrm {d}^{2} \mathrm {t / d} \lambda^{2}}{(\mathrm {d} t / \mathrm {d} \lambda )^{2}} \mathbf {g}_{\mathrm {j k}} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}^{\mathbf {k}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = 0 .$$Combine this with the time part of the geodesic equation$$\frac {(\mathrm {d}^{2} \mathrm {t} / \mathrm {d} \lambda^{2})}{(\mathrm {d t} / \mathrm {d} \lambda )^{2}} = - 2 \Gamma_{0 \mathrm {k} 0} \frac {\mathrm {d x}^{\mathrm {k}} / \mathrm {d t}}{\mathbf {g}_{0 0}}$$and use the expression for the $\Gamma$ 's in terms of the metric to get$$\gamma_{j k} \frac {\mathrm {d}^{2} x^{k}}{\mathrm {d} t^{2}} + \frac {1}{2} \left(\gamma_{j k, l} + \gamma_{j l, k} - \gamma_{k l, j}\right) \frac {\mathrm {d} x^{k}}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {\mathrm {d} x^{\ell}}{\mathrm {d} t} = 0, \tag {2}$$where $\gamma_{jk} \equiv -g_{jk} / g_{00}$ . Notice that this is just a geodesic equation with affine parameter $t$ in the 3-dimensional manifold with metric $\gamma_{jk}$ . From Equation (1) it is clear then that the solution of Equation (2) extremizes $\int dt$ ; Fermat's principle holds. [See also C. Moller, The Theory of Relativity (Oxford University Press, 2nd ed., 1972), p. 308.]
